1: A shadow: The superlative title shir hashirim — the Song of Songs — marks this as the peak of the wisdom tradition's meditation on love. Its canonical placement among the wisdom books signals that the love it describes is not peripheral to Israel's theology but central to it. The NT's bridegroom theology (John 3:29; Matthew 9:15; Ephesians 5:25-32; Revelation 19:7-9) draws on this tradition: Christ is the bridegroom whose love for the church the Song shadows. The Song anticipates not an abstraction but a person.
2: A revelation of God: The Bride's longing — 'Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth' — is the soul's cry for direct intimacy with God, not mediated through law or intermediary but immediate and personal. Paul's prayer in Ephesians 3:17-19 is the NT's answer: that Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith, that you may know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. The longing the Song voices is the longing the Spirit creates in every believer (Romans 8:26-27).
3: A shadow: 'Your name is like perfume poured out' — the name of the beloved is not merely a title but an essence, a fragrance that draws. The NT's fulfillment is the name of Jesus: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9). The fragrance metaphor returns in 2 Corinthians 2:15: 'we are the aroma of Christ to God.' The young women's love drawn by the name anticipates the elect drawn to Christ (John 6:44: no one can come to me unless the Father draws him).
4: A shadow: 'Draw me, let us run' — the initiative belongs to the beloved, not the bride; she asks to be drawn. Jesus's word in John 12:32 — 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself' — directly answers this petition: the drawing is accomplished through the cross. The entry 'into his chambers' anticipates the heavenly dwelling Jesus prepares (John 14:2-3) and the eschatological feast (Revelation 19:9).
5: A revelation of God: 'I am dark and lovely' — the bride's self-description holds together two truths that seem contradictory: she is darkened by sun and labor (Song of Solomon 1:6) yet loved and found beautiful. The NT's application is the church: sinful in herself, yet declared beautiful by the one who loved her and gave himself for her (Ephesians 5:25-27). The juxtaposition of darkness and loveliness is the aesthetic form of justification: the church is simultaneously sinful and beloved.
6: A revelation of God: The bride was made keeper of other vineyards while her own went unattended — a picture of the soul distracted from its own spiritual care by external demands. The NT parallel is Mary and Martha (Luke 10:38-42): Martha busy with service, missing the one thing necessary. Christ's response — 'you are anxious about many things; one thing is needed' — is the answer to the bride's complaint. The Beloved's prior claim on the soul precedes and grounds all other service.
7: A shadow: The bride asks where the shepherd rests his flock at midday — she wants to be where he is, not wandering among the flocks of others. This anticipates the disciple's question to Jesus: 'Rabbi, where are you staying?' (John 1:38), and Jesus's answer: 'Come and you will see.' The desire to be where Christ is rather than merely receiving his benefits is the mark of genuine faith — Paul's 'my desire is to depart and be with Christ' (Philippians 1:23).
8: A shadow: 'Follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your young goats near the shepherds' tents.' The bride is directed to find the shepherd by following the flock already gathered around him. This reflects the NT's ecclesiology: the individual believer finds Christ by joining the assembled community that follows him (Hebrews 10:24-25). The path to intimacy with Christ runs through the people of Christ.
9: A revelation of God: The beloved is compared to a mare harnessed to Pharaoh's chariots — not the dainty ornamental horse of peacetime but the powerful animal that disrupts battle lines. The comparison speaks to the effect the beloved has on the lover: overwhelming, disruptive, captivating. God's delight in his people is depicted throughout Scripture as a love that is not cool or distant but deeply moved (Zephaniah 3:17: he will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing).
10: A revelation of God: The beloved's beauty is adorned with jewelry — cheeks beautiful with ornaments, neck with strings of beads. The theme of God adorning his people is developed in Ezekiel 16:11-14 (I adorned you with ornaments, put bracelets on your wrists, a chain on your neck) and finds its eschatological expression in the bride of the Lamb 'clothed with fine linen, bright and pure' (Revelation 19:8). The adornment is not the bride's own achievement but the gift of the lover.
11: A revelation of God: 'We will make for you gold ornaments studded with silver' — the lover promises future adornment even more glorious than present gifts. The eschatological note: what Christ promises to the church is still ahead. The present partial beauty (1 Corinthians 13:12: now we see in a mirror dimly) will be replaced by the full adornment of the new creation. 'It has not yet appeared what we will be, but we know that when he appears we will be like him' (1 John 3:2).
12: A shadow: 'While the king reclined at his table, my perfume spread its fragrance.' The king at table — the meal scene — anticipates the eschatological banquet (Isaiah 25:6; Luke 14:15-24; Revelation 19:9). The bride's fragrance filling the room while the king reclines suggests the mutual delight of the covenant relationship at its deepest: Christ and the church at table together. 'Behold, I stand at the door and knock. If anyone opens it, I will come in and eat with him' (Revelation 3:20).
13: A shadow: The sachet of myrrh resting between the bride's breasts is a constant fragrance she carries with her. Myrrh in the OT is the burial spice (Genesis 37:25; Exodus 30:23) as well as the coronation anointing. In the NT, myrrh marks both the birth of Jesus (Matthew 2:11: the magi's gift) and his burial (John 19:39: Nicodemus brought myrrh and aloes). The bride's carrying of myrrh against her heart foreshadows the church's carrying the cross of Christ — the atonement — as her central, constant treasure (1 Corinthians 2:2: I decided to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ and him crucified).
14: A shadow: The cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi — En-gedi was an oasis in the Judean wilderness, a place of lush growth in an arid landscape. The image of the beloved as an oasis in the desert is the OT's pervasive image for YHWH's provision in the wilderness (Exodus 17:6; Isaiah 35:1-7; Song of Solomon 43:19-20). Jesus applies the same image to himself: 'If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink' (John 7:37); 'I am the vine' (John 15:1). The cluster of blossoms is beauty and sweetness in a place of barrenness.
15: A revelation of God: 'How beautiful you are, my love! Your eyes are like doves.' The lover's repeated praise of the beloved's beauty — 'how beautiful' — is the divine delight in the people God has redeemed. Dove-eyes in the ancient world conveyed purity and singularity of focus. Jesus says 'the eye is the lamp of the body' (Matthew 6:22) — a single eye focused on Christ is the covenant loyalty the Song celebrates. Christ's delight in the church's love for him (Revelation 2:4: you have abandoned the love you had at first) is the reverse of this verse's joy.
16: A revelation of God: The bride praises the groom's beauty and declares their shared dwelling place — a bower of green. The mutuality: she finds him beautiful as he finds her. The church's growing capacity to see Christ as beautiful — not merely useful or powerful but personally lovely — is the mark of spiritual maturity (Philippians 3:8: I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord).
17: A shadow: Cedar beams, cypress rafters — the finest timber of the ancient world (cf. the temple in 1 Kings 6:15-18). The lovers' dwelling is built with the same materials as the house of God. The image points forward to the new creation where the dwelling of God is with his people (Revelation 21:3): what the Song envisions as an intimate dwelling of lovers is the eschatological telos of redemption history — God and his people at home with one another.
